posts.name: Steve G0th
posts.cooked:
I love experiencing new enjoyable wine discoveries. I have found that wine geeks’ excitement for wine can be invaluable.

So I want to know, what wine gets you excited? What wine makes you prepare a whole meal around it. What wine do you shout about to all your wine geek friends? What wine makes you look forward to the next moment it is poured into your glass? What wine makes you feel like everything in your life has achieved perfect harmony?

This could be a specific bottle, producer, year in a region, etc. Excitement can be contagious so spread it around!

Thank you in advance.

posts.name: Zachary Mancini
posts.cooked:
Beta/Jasud
Vice Versa
Kinsman
Diconstanzo
Macdonald

posts.name: JamesG
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Some wines that I own a fair bit of but which really do get me excited to pop, are:

Vatan
Magdelaine
Rougeard
Jamet
Juge
Anything 1998 Pomerol - so in the zone and awesome

Wish that I had more Allemand. And wish that i had Rayas. A 2008 that I popped with some buddies 2 years ago was beyond spectacular. That wine makes an evening, a weekend, a true memory. I chose a Michelin 2 in Nîmes, France, back in May only because they had 2 vintages of Rayas on the menu (and then funny enough, it was all gone when I got there, but I had a most excellent 2010 Pignan).

I’ve also had two guy’s trips recently with amazing wines, and that 2005 Vatan both times was WOTW. Another spectacular, singular wine.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked: I find new reasons to be excited virtually every day. Quinta da Biaia is producing unexpectedly fantastic wines in the Beira Interior. Bairrada’s Baga based reds have never been so stylistically diverse. Textura Wines have brought to reality the dream of Burgundian-Barolian expressions of the terroir in Serra da Estrela. Not only are the Vinhos Verdes producing serious wine, they’re producing serious bubbles. Previously undiscovered old vines are producing profound wines n the Tejo region (the Tomaralmá project). The Portuguese wine revolution is frenetic and seemingly unstoppable. Everyone has a project, a new idea or at least a word to say.

posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked:
A Tribute to Grace — all of her Grenache are wonderful and just got the new releases this week.

Conterno Gattinara — fun to drink Gattinara as its been years.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Hey Steve, I would be remiss if I did not also tell you that I geek out on value plays as well, and actually feel like they are a more important part of my collection since I enjoy wines every evening.

Right now I am drinking a wine that hits every note that you were seeking in your original post, and yet it is wildly affordable. I have posted about this wine many times before, but in 2019 it seems like it just took on another level. It’s the 2019 Roilette Griffe Du Marquis. This is probably one of the better Beaujolais wines that I have ever had. It’s drinking spectacularly right now, and I also think, as typical with this estate, it can mature for a long time. Some might say it commands time. I have been buying this special cuvee by Roilette every year since the inaugural release in 2009, and I have heard the 2021 is special as well.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Steve_G0th:

I am a big fan of Champagne and follow the long running Champagne thread so I am not surprised with your response . Are there a few Champagnes across different price points that you are particularly happy with at the moment?

I’m not Warren, he’s a real champagne expert, I’m just a drinker. But for me, it appears to be Cristal. I’ve been at three big champagne weekends events recently, and the Cristal shined for me every time across multiple vintages, especially the 2008. Now again, bear in mind that I have zero expertise with champagne, but I do know what I like.

Frankly these are more exciting than trophy names I already know. I was very excited to try my first Extradimensional wines and hope my mind is blown with some of these new ones as much as I was by Hardy’s.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: I’m excited about anything I’m interested in and have never tried. Once I’ve tried a wine, it’s very rare for me to be “excited” about subsequent bottles unless either (1) the first bottle was jaw-dropping amazing or (2) a lot of time has passed since the previous bottle and I previously thought it had room to improve with age.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: andrew messenger
posts.cooked:
I get excited about wines that I’ve read great reviews about especially from CellarTracker and I was able to secure a couple of bottles of those wines.

I recently had a 2007 Jaffurs Vernas Vineyard Syrah. I read about it on CellarTracker and was very excited to try a bottle. I previously tried Upslope and was very pleased with it. I actually think the Vernas was the better of the two. What a great wine. I doubt I would get the same excitement with the second try but that’s the beauty of first time experiences.

I tend to be a Francophile, but trying a wine like this with a fantastic QPR is eye-opening and exciting

Ran into a mutual acquaintance the other day and he said Kevin was out of the wine business. Doing something like foraging abalone on the coast or something wild like that, I forget.

posts.name: Steve G0th
posts.cooked:
So many interesting recommendations. Lots of interesting wines to seek out. I have to see what the excitement about Kobayashi is and just placed my first order.

Keep them coming, please!

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked:
This is kind of where I’m at as well. There are basically 2 categories

The first is new things I haven’t tried yet. I have an 04 Rayas lined up this year along with a Grange des Peres white. There is a lunch on the horizon with a bottle of Prévost Iota and Coche Meursault which could be awesome. I can’t wait to try @William_Kelley ’s Gevrey Aux Etelois but will probably wait a while on those

The other kind of excitement is generated from trying a bottle that I’ve cellared (and ideally have others of) in effort to follow its arc and get a feel for a) how are wines I’ve cellared performing and b) are my tasting impressions accurate. I also love trying a benchmark wine a second time, to see if my initial impression holds up, and in some cases my palate has changed

With regard to producers, I’ll confess to being excited by hyped up bottlings, the sort of things you see on IG. Always get butterflies for Ramonet, Raveneau and Selosse, Bernaudeau and Keller, Jamet and Allemand, anything Dujac, would love to try more Mugneret Gibourg. The usual suspects.

reply_to_user.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Emery Adoradio
posts.cooked: I continue to be excited about Ippolito Cirò Classico Superiore Ripe del Falco Riserva. I learned of this wine in Robert Camuto’s South of Somewhere (excellent read of the author’s tour of southern Italian wine regions, in the vein of Kermit Lynch’s Adventures on the Wine Route). This wine, 100% gaglioppo, is worth seeking out. A modern style of Cirò, but fabulous and has tremendous longevity. I’ve greatly enjoyed the '10, '12, and '15.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The 2021 Etelois is pretty open actually, not a crime to try one young in that vintage! That’s why we released it before 2020.

posts.name: Jim Petrila
posts.cooked:
I will pile on re Kobayashi. The 2020 syrah I opened was a really electric wine and I can’t wait to follow these over years and vintages.

Another wine that got me excited recently was a 2017 B&T Glantenay clos chenes. A precise and complex wine beyond what I was expecting when I opened.

Finally, a 1980 Diamond Creek Volcanic Hill earlier this week. So fresh after 40+ years and so beautiful to drink. Really got me excited for the producers in Napa now who look to these 70s/80s cabs as their lode star.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
On the high end I tend to get most excited by a lot of the same stuff mentioned here: anything made by Rayas, Jamet or Chave with age, old Burgundy with age from producers like Dujac, Trapet, Drouhin, Roumier, Chevillon, Gouges, Lafarge. Of course old Bordeaux is a mainstay. Love me some Pichon Lalande, Latour, Cantemerle, Lanessan, Conseilante, etc…

An area I think is underappreciated is well aged Southern Rhone. I had bafflingly good Beaucastels from 1980 and 1985 this summer. I’ve opened some great 1994s in the past couple years. Had some good 1978s and 1983s as well. From these older and cooler vintages, which were before global warming really kicked in, plenty of alcohols in the 13.5% - 14% range. I’ve had many occasions where when tasted blind people have guessed they were good Burgundy. You can spend a fraction of the price of old Burgundy and still get a beautiful lighter/elegant experience.

While I’ve never had the opportunity to buy Vatan, I’ve been wowed by Francois Cotat and Didier Dageneau lately each with some light age on them.

I’ve also been quite enamoured by Huet, both young and with some age.

An area that generated a lot of buzz that I never really understood was Mencia from Bierzo, but the Envinate Lousas reall changed that for me, especially the Seoane bottling.

I’ve also really enjoyed Canary Islands reds. Envinate and Suertes del Marqués have been lovely. lighter bodied like Pinot Noir, but the peppery, smoky aromatics of Syrah.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked: Domaine Des Tuileries - Especially their Meunier. Small grower champagne that actually makes good no dosage wine. Their 2019 Acacia punches above its price point. Shoutout to @D_Pennet for the reco. I also tried their Empreintes, their 100% pinot noir, and it was excellent. I typically prefer dosed champagnes, but these are very good. The Acacia is one of the better sub-$90 bottles of champagne I’ve had this year.
